figures.caption: Figure 11: Korean translation prompt. Prompt template used to translate English queries into Korean (ko) when constructing BioASQ-ENKB5 and Hotpot-ENKB5.
Figure 12: Thai translation prompt. Prompt template used to translate English queries into Thai (th) when constructing BioASQ-ENKB5 and Hotpot-ENKB5.

Table 1: Performance comparison in BioASQ-ENKB5, with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct as the backbone, and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct as the teacher. Bold is the best, and underline is the runner-up. The teacher (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct) and base (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) rows are reference-only and are not included in the best/runner-up ranking.
Figure 15: The language control instructions of the Prompt-Control baseline [56, 65].
Figure 25: Case study. Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct as the backbone, Vietnamese as language, in HotpotENKB5.
Figure 22: Case study. Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct as the backbone, Japanese as language, in HotpotENKB5.
Figure 23: Case study. Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct as the backbone, Korean as language, in HotpotENKB5.

Table 10: Performance comparison in Hotpot-ENKB5, with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct as the backbone, and Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct as the teacher. Bold is the best, and underline is the runner-up. The teacher (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct) and base (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) rows are reference-only and are not ranked. See the note in Section D.5 on the language-consistency plateau in this table.

Figure 8: Qualitative case study (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct backbone, Indonesian, Hotpot-ENKB5). Highlighting is consistent across panels: red marks language drift (Indonesian slipping into copied English spans) or evidence-unsupported content, and green marks correct, evidence-grounded content in the target language. Baseline systems drift and latch onto a salient but unsupported entity, whereas TR-RAG stays in fluent Indonesian and returns the entity justified by the full evidence chain. Best viewed in color.

Table 3: Generalization to naturally multilingual MKQA with English-evidence (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct backbone). Per-language Composite, Qwen3-Next-80B Judge, and cross-family Llama-3.3-70B Judge are reported. Bold/underline denote best/runner-up.

Figure 1: Overview. (a) English-evidence cross-lingual RAG: queries/answers are in a target language while retrieved passages are English. (The panel labels the retrieved documents “Top-K” in the generic retrieval sense; throughout the text their count is denoted M , as K is reserved for the number of on-policy rollouts per input.) (b) Two prefix-dependent failure modes—language drift and evidence-use failures—induce two training obstacles: prefix mismatch under fixed-trajectory supervision and high-variance optimization under sequence-level rewards. (c) TR-RAG couples on-policy RL with on-policy distillation: a frozen teacher is queried on student-visited prefixes to produce a dense reverse-KL anchor KL(πθ∥πT ) (no teacher rollouts). “T.A.” denotes this teacher anchor.

Table 9: Performance comparison in BioASQ-ENKB5, with Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 as the backbone, and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 as the teacher. Bold is the best, and underline is the runner-up. The teacher (Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507) and base (Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507) rows are reference-only and are not ranked.

Table 11: Performance comparison in Hotpot-ENKB5, with Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 as the backbone, and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 as the teacher. Bold is the best, and underline is the runner-up. The teacher (Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507) and base (Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507) rows are reference-only and are not ranked. See the note in Section D.5 on the language-consistency plateau in this table.
Figure 9: Indonesian translation prompt. Prompt template used to translate English queries into Indonesian (id) when constructing BioASQ-ENKB5 and Hotpot-ENKB5.

Figure 14: LLM-judge prompt. We use a rubric-based evaluator prompt to score student answers for RAG, conditioned on the question q, retrieved English context D, reference answer a⋆ (training only), and student output y. The prompt instructs the judge to assess (i) faithfulness (hallucination checking against D), (ii) correctness (alignment with a⋆, with entity focus for BioASQ and multihop completeness for HotpotQA), and (iii) conciseness. The judge returns a scalar score in [0, 1] following a strict rubric (e.g., 1.0/0.8/0.5/0.2/0.0) and outputs it in a constrained XML format for reliable parsing.
Figure 13: Vietnamese translation prompt. Prompt template used to translate English queries into Vietnamese (vi) when constructing BioASQ-ENKB5 and Hotpot-ENKB5.
Figure 10: Japanese translation prompt. Prompt template used to translate English queries into Japanese (ja) when constructing BioASQ-ENKB5 and Hotpot-ENKB5.

Figure 2: Framework. (a) Inputs: a multilingual query q and English-only evidence D. (b) The student samples on-policy rollouts and receives the task reward. (c) The teacher is queried only on student prefixes to compute a KL anchor (no teacher rollouts). (d) The student is updated with policy gradients and a frozen-teacher reverse-KL anchor.
